messages: Update: over the weekend I injured my left ankle and the doctor advised that it shouldn’t be bearing weight for at least two months.
Also, my parents unexpectedly sold the car last week, so my regular access to a vehicle for practice is gone for now.
Taken together, that means the manual-focused, in-car practice plan we discussed is no longer feasible at the moment.
Given this change, what is realistic for my original three-month target—should I be postponing the practical test now?
Are there effective alternatives that let me keep progressing without actually getting behind the wheel, at least until I’m mobile again?
If I keep the current test date in ten weeks, is there any reasonable way to make meaningful progress toward being test-ready under these new constraints?
Would an investment in simulator time and extra theory/hazard-perception practice be a good stopgap while I recover?
If I eventually switch to training in an automatic rather than the stick we talked about, will the early manual-focused study still help, or would much of it be wasted?
Are there low-cost options—community programs, patronized instructors, or test centers with rental automatics—that could let me get a few supervised hours when I’m cleared to drive?
I don’t want months of preparation to evaporate; what’s the most efficient way to retain progress during recovery and loss of vehicle access?
To be concrete, assume I won’t be able to do regular driving for the next six to eight weeks and that my usual practice vehicle won’t be available during that time.
Also assume my practical test is still scheduled in roughly ten weeks and moving it would add cost and delay I’d rather avoid if there’s a reasonable workaround.
Given those two limits, which activities should take absolute priority so I don’t lose momentum toward the license?

Please outline a week-by-week plan that maximizes readiness without regular in-car practice, including when I should aim to book any in-person lessons or a mock test as soon as driving becomes possible.
Be specific about low-cost resources, simulator options, or local community programs I can use immediately, and when I should transition back to actual on-road training.
Finally, give me a short checklist of immediate actions I can take this week that respect my mobility and access limits but keep me steadily moving toward being test-ready.
